replication_study.hypothesis: In five-player public-infrastructure games, the interaction between player position (coded 1 = most-upstream … 5 = most-downstream) and the lagged share of downloaded resource from the previous round is positive: downstream (larger position value) players invest more in the shared infrastructure when they received a higher share of the resource flow in the prior round.

data_plan.source_type: Laboratory experiment database (tokens-for-bandwidth game conducted at Arizona State University)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: All sessions; rounds 3-12 retained for analysis (rounds > 2 as in original Janssen et al. 2011 specification).
data_plan.access_details: Data are provided in /app/data/Full_long.dta inside the replication bundle (no external request required).

qualification.explanation: The dataset is exactly the raw experimental output on which the focal claim was originally estimated, allowing a computational (re-analysis) replication of the reported interaction effect.
qualification.similarity_to_original: Variables playerinvestment, playerrole, playerdownloaded_files, playercollected_tokens, groupbandwidth, Session, groupid_in_subsession, etc., appear in both the Stata script of Janssen et al. (Analysis.do in replication_data) and Table 8 of the published paper (see original_paper.pdf, p. 13).
qualification.deviation_from_original: No new experimental sessions are added; this is a *direct* replication on the same raw data but executed in Python instead of Stata, thus testing computational robustness rather than external-sample generalisability.

data_plan.notes: The file is in long format (participant-round). Two derived variables (shr = lagged share of downloads; pstnshr = interaction) are constructed exactly as in the Stata do-file. Encoding is Stata-13; pandas.read_stata requires the optional dependency pyreadstat.

steps: Create analysis variables following the original Stata do-file (see Analysis.do): nvst, pstn, rnd, shr, pstnshr, etc.
Compute group-round sums to obtain share variables, then create lagged share within participant.
Fit a mixed-effects linear model with random intercept for experimental group (grp = Session*100 + groupid_in_subsession).
Extract coefficient, standard error, and p-value for the interaction term pstnshr.
planned_method.models: Linear mixed-effects model (random intercept for group) via statsmodels MixedLM.
planned_method.outcome_variable: nvst – tokens invested in public infrastructure in round t.
planned_method.independent_variables: pstn (position), shr (lagged share of downloads), pstnshr (interaction), rnd (round trend).
planned_method.control_variables: Linear time trend (rnd); random intercepts for group (grp).

planned_estimation_and_test.test: t-test on fixed-effect coefficient (two-sided, df via REML).

planned_method.missing_data_handling: List-wise deletion after variable construction (drop rows with NA on any model variable).
planned_method.inference_criteria: Replication is successful if the pstn×shr coefficient is positive and statistically significant at α = 0.05 (two-tailed), matching the sign and significance reported by Janssen et al. (2011, Table 8, column 3).

files.analysis_replication.py: Pure-Python translation of Analysis.do. Loads Full_long.dta, recreates variables, fits mixed-effects model, prints and saves results.

codebase.notes: All I/O paths are rooted at /app/data to satisfy execution policy. The script is self-contained; no Stata runtime is required.

analysis.instructions: Within the docker container, run:  python /app/data/original/30/input/replication_data/analysis_replication.py
analysis.comparison_metrics: 1) Sign of pstn×shr coefficient; 2) p-value (<0.05); 3) Magnitude close to original (≈3.836).
